transcript.transcript: All right, so the recorder is playing. We will go ahead and get started. Thank you so much for sitting down with me today. Today is Tuesday, May 8, 2023. My name is HannaLore Hein, State Historian for the Idaho State Historical Society, and I am meeting with Dr. Christine Hahn, Idaho State Epidemiologist and Director of the Idaho Division on Public Health with the Idaho Department of Health and Welfare, to conduct an oral history related to Dr. Hahn's experience as state epidemiologist and an administrator during COVID-19. This oral history is part of a larger COVID-19 oral history project supported by Governor Brad Little's Leading Idaho Initiative and is an investment in Idaho's historic record. I will begin with questions general in nature that become more specific as we continue, and I may reorder some questions to facilitate the flow of conversation, or I may ask some unscripted follow-up questions. But before we begin, can I ask that you please state and spell your first and last name for me? Sure. It is Christine Hahn, C-H-R-I-S-T-I-N-E, and last name is spelled H-A-H-N. Great. Well, thank you so much. We are sitting here at the Department of Health and Welfare in downtown Boise to conduct this oral history. I am really looking forward to our conversation today. From the research that I did to prepare for our conversation today, I learned that you completed your fellowship at Duke University Hospital in 1995, and that you did spend some time in a fellowship at the CDC before you became the Idaho State Epidemiologist in 1997. I was hoping that you could tell us just a little bit about your background, where you are from originally, and some of those early educational opportunities that you had. Sure, yes. I am from Kalamazoo, Michigan, so it is in the southwest corner of Michigan, and my dad was a history professor, my mom was a stay-at-home mom, and really no medical people in the family. I do not have relatives that I knew of at the time that were in the medical field. I got a scholarship to a college, Kalamazoo College, that to get the scholarship, I had to major in the sciences, which I was interested in anyway. I always loved science. That started me down the path of the sciences and decided during college that I wanted to apply to medical school. I loved the biology and learning about the way the body works and the way the cells work and all that and went to medical school at Michigan State and just fell in love with infectious diseases from the get-go. Fascinating how you know different organisms interact with each other, not always in a positive way, and I just always thought it was the most interesting area of medicine. After doing my internal medicine residency at the Mayo Clinic in Minnesota, then I went down to North Carolina to study infectious diseases at Duke. Part of that time I spent a year in Tanzania in East Africa studying, trying to find an HIV vaccine. We were doing some work that we hoped would help us find a vaccine for HIV and here we are now over twenty years later and there still is no HIV vaccine. Needless to say the study was not a success but I learned a lot and it was an incredibly valuable experience and it really helped point me towards public health. When I came back from Tanzania, I think I was convinced I wanted to work in public health rather than clinical medicine and applied for the CDC program and just decided that was definitely the route I wanted to go. The CDC is the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention and that is in Atlanta, Georgia? Correct. I was actually doing a program with them but I only spent the first month in Atlanta and then they stationed me as it were here in Idaho. I was able to choose where I wanted to go but I selected— I really wanted— I had never been west of the Rockies at all, not even to travel. It was a great— I was excited about coming out here and getting to know a large rural state which is in some ways weather-wise close to Michigan. I love the four seasons, I love skiing, but also I was just excited about the state and some of the challenges they face here. Do you remember what the projects were that you got to work on as part of that fellowship? Yes, it was— the first thing that happened when I got here, there was a Shigella outbreak in eastern Idaho, and it was actually near Henry's Fork area, very famous fly-fishing area in eastern Idaho not far from Yellowstone. Shigella is a bacteria that causes nasty diarrhea and that type of thing and a lot of people were coming down sick with it. I was sent out there about three weeks after I got to Idaho to work on that outbreak and we figured out that a well had gotten contaminated. The water, it was in the water so it was a really— and there were over 200 cases confirmed. Of course, this being a hot tourist area, it was really important, and I was really glad to be able to help the local health department figure that out and get that stopped. I also got to work on, I remember going up north and working on lead contamination up there in Kellogg area where they have had a long history there of lead contamination in their soils after a, I think it was called a bag fire where the mining industry, they usually run the products through this filter of kind of set up as bags. It had burned in it. They continued to spew lead into the air for many years after that. It did not get figured out until later that that had happened. I worked on lead poisoning a lot and also other infectious disease outbreaks. We had an E. Coli outbreak, those kind of things. It was really a, whatever was needed, I was able to help with. It was really, really interesting and I felt like I learned a lot. You mentioned that you were really drawn to this idea of public health as opposed to clinical health and I was hoping you could give your definition of what public health means to you. Yes, that is a great question. I think what I found was when I was starting to enter the field of infectious diseases, so mid-90s, that was when AIDS was in crisis. There was . not good treatment yet for AIDS and people were dying in the hospital. Incredible people working hard on that problem. But it was— I kept thinking it was so different than what I had envisioned, which was more about working with people to get them vaccinated or make sure they understood about clean water. I think I just had a different vision. Maybe my interest in global and international health, I had kind of a different vision for the way I wanted to head versus working in the hospital and just doing a lot of intensive care which is what infectious disease was at the time. I thought it was gonna be just a two-year, "Oh I think I need a break. I am going to go spend some time at CDC, maybe get that out of my system." But then I decided "No, I really like this. This is the way I want to— this is where I want to stay." When you were in Idaho, is that when the state epidemiologist position became available or opened? What was the process like to apply for that job? Yes, the epidemiologist is the one that recruited me, the previous state epidemiologist. While I was still here training, he left to take a position in New Hampshire, actually became the state epidemiologist of New Hampshire. I finished out my training program without my mentor and then when I was wrapping it up, they offered me the position. I must have had to go through some competitive application process. It has been too long for me to remember the details, but I was able to apply for and get the job at that time. They knew me for two years by then and I think they knew that I was a good fit. Excellent. Excellent. Thank you. [pause] You clearly have a long tenure here with the Department of Health and Welfare. As a department, it has existed under various names over the course of the better part of almost a hundred years, more than a hundred years. Could you talk a little bit about what some of the core programs are for the department now since you have been here since the late 90s? Right. I will start off talking about the department and talk about the Division of Public Health. As you know, the Department of Health and Welfare is divided into several divisions. The biggest one, I think budgetary-wise and even I think employee-wise, is Medicaid. Huge program. Obviously offers health insurance essentially to a large number of Idahoans, very important program. I view Medicaid as really part of public health in the sense that they are allowing people access to health care, immunizations, health screenings, et cetera. Very important division. We also have the division of family and children services. We have licensing and certification that licenses and certifies like nursing homes around the state and other entities, agencies. There is a lot of great work in the department. The Division of Public Health is pretty small compared to some of those big divisions, but we always say small but mighty. We try to— we consider our patient, if you will, as the population of the state. We try to serve everyone in the state, mostly by working with the local health districts. We have seven local public health districts in the state. We do not control them. They are independent county-based entities. But we work through them and with them to implement things like health screenings, immunizations, education for people, classes like fit and fall proof classes for seniors where they do exercises so they do not fall and become injured. It is— and then things like food inspection, safety inspections for restaurants. The health districts carry out some of those but under the with some funding support and guidance from the state. The Division of Public Health has lots of other programs encouraging cancer screenings, things like just kind of mentally going around the room. We obviously have our immunization program. We have a refugee health screening program. New refugees in the state, as they arrive, they all are screened. First of all, for their safety, to make sure that if they have a health condition that needs to be treated, they are treated right away. And then also for the safety of the public coming in, so they are not worried, "Oh, what are they bringing in with them?" These folks are screened, they are tested, they are treated if necessary. Then we have vital statistics is really important, of course. As a historian, I am sure you appreciate that. [laughter] Yes, yes. There are record keepers for births, deaths, marriages, et cetera. They are within our division as well. Specifically in your role as state epidemiologist, what are the duties that come to you on a daily basis or you know monthly cadence even that constitute what you get to do on a daily in your world. Yes, the role of state epidemiologist is interesting because it is not defined in statute or anything about what exactly I have to do. But I work with an organization of all the fifty states called the Council of State and Territorial Epidemiologists, and we all work together to say, "Yes,, what is our main function?" The way I see it is I am someone here with a medical background, but also hopefully that broad public health view. I think of it as we track data, we make sure surveillance is in place, kind of like the vital statistics for diseases, not for deaths or births, but for how many cases of salmonella were there. How many cases of TB did we have last year? Really making sure we can accurately track those numbers. But tracking is not enough, of course. The other part is analyzing that data, which would be, "Oh, the number of cases of TB are going up." And then investigating those trends and lastly, responding. That is how I think of it, like a sequence. We have to have those systems in place. We have to look at the data. We have to understand the data, investigate more, or do special studies if needed, and then ultimately either respond ourselves or make recommendations. For example, putting press releases out to the public— "Hey, this is going up, make sure you are immunized, or "This is going up, make sure you are washing your hands." There is— or "STDs, make sure you are practicing safe sex behaviors." That kind of thing. It is that spectrum and we have lots of people that work on those topics, but I feel like as state epidemiologist, I am the one that tries to be the mouthpiece. For example, if we need to talk to the media or maybe help write press releases about the subject that hopefully people can understand. Excellent. Now that we have got a baseline for how some of the department and the divisions work and how your role works within that, we are going to jump into the meat of the interview, which is, of course, this moment in time when the COVID-19 pandemic arrived here in Idaho. On March 13, 2020, that is when we had our first lab confirmed case of the coronavirus. On that day, Governor Brad Little issued a state of emergency so that the state could begin to prepare for this pandemic. I want you to try to put yourself back into that moment in time. Can you remember when you first started to hear about this pandemic and what were your sources and information? Yes, I do remember that it was in late 2019 there was information circulating through some of the listservs and information sources that we use in public health and epidemiology just to keep track of all sorts of things. Avian flu over here, an outbreak of Ebola in Africa. We were always kind of trying to keep our eyes open for these things so that we were aware of something that might be coming. There was discussion, I want to say it was in December, November, December, it was pretty late in the year, but we did hear that there was a possible SARS-like virus in China. I had been already in my position when we had SARS 1. I want to say that was 2003, if I remember correctly. I remember that being alarming. We watched it closely. There were cases that came over into Canada. Then it fizzled and just went away. Nobody ever quite understood why that happened. Because we were "Oh my gosh, this is happening. It has jumped from China to North America. What is going to happen?" There were cases spread in hospitals. It sounded very, very worrisome, and then it just went away. Even though we heard about, "Oh, there is another SARS virus in China," obviously, we could not have anticipated what was going to happen. But we had that mix of concern and, "Oh, well, if it is anything like last time, it is going to be nothing." That was when I officially started hearing stuff about it, but, as you know, that China did not officially report to WHO until December 31st, 2019. It was not really until January of 2020 that we started getting more official— WHO came out with their announcements, CDC, news story started really picking it up. Before Governor Little issued this declaration, what kind of conversations were you having internally with your leadership team and with Director Jeppesen and with some of the other administrators of the divisions with regard to what you were hearing from those sources? Yes, I think we probably did not start having those conversations until 2020, right? In 2019, it seemed very far away and remote chance that really impact us. But in 2020, once WHO came out with an announcement about it and we realized that this was certainly of concern, we did start discussing. Again, another disease we would track very similarly would be avian influenza. Every time there is a scary new avian flu, we start to have meetings about it. We did have some discussions. I am trying to remember a specific meeting. I would have to probably go for that and look at my notes, but I do have notes that we did have meetings. By March 13, we knew, of course, it was already the United States, Washington State having the first cases, which is right next door, right? That really got our attention. I think as soon as Washington announced their first case, it kicked into high gear at that point. Our preparedness program started pulling together a response group. I am forgetting what we exactly called it, because we quickly realized we should not be meeting in person. We had a couple of meetings where we all got in a room together, and they are like, "Wait a minute. This is an infectious disease, and if it is starting to spread in the United States, we need to be careful." But we did have some meetings, and we started to update the director. I do have some— I did find when I was just getting ready for today's discussion, prior to the first meeting of the governor's working group that he created, I did have several updates I gave just to the governor and a small group prior to March 13. I know we were already having those conversations. Did the governor reach out to you directly for that or did that request come via Director Jeppesen? Yes, I think in the beginning— I knew the governor a little bit from I had been working on some of the opioid overdose related issues and some other things where I had met the governor. But I am sure that in the beginning all those requests would have come through Director Jeppesen because the governor would not have known exactly who would be the best people to talk with initially. Knowing that the SARS-1 issue fizzled, did this one cause fear? Did you feel fearful? I did not. I think, certainly I would definitely say I was concerned. But having had that experience that we had, I felt it was unlikely— honestly, I did not anticipate it would become what it did. I thought, "Well, we have seen this before. We got to watch it. We certainly do not want to be asleep at the wheel." But I felt— I just was not aware that SARS could do this because of how it had fizzled before. I thought it was going to maybe cause a few cases and then potentially die out again. Great. Clearly, with the declaration of the emergency, the following week or within two weeks, Governor Little began his plan to close things down. He issued a stay-at-home order. Businesses that were deemed non-essential were asked to close. Most employees were put on plans to kind of work telework. State employees, that is, were put on telework plans. How did the arrival of that pandemic shift your work in the first several weeks in March, April, May of 2020? Right. I would say it went from maybe the beginning where we were keeping an eye. Maybe 10% of my time was spent on some conference calls with CSTE or our professional organization of state epidemiologists, and CDC maybe providing updates to suddenly it was 90, 95% of my time. It was really a zero to a hundred, or five miles per hour to ninety nine miles per hour. It just felt like a sudden shift. It was, as you mentioned, I think what took up a lot of our time in the beginning, not only everybody, of course, wants to be updated, so you felt like you are spending a lot of time— I did not mind those at all. That is what we do is try to communicate. We spent half our time absorbing new information, being on calls with CDC, reading things, reading materials, and then the other half trying to make that digestible and put it out, whether it was to the public, whether it was for the governor, whether it was for leadership inside the medical community or in the department. I would say almost all our time. And then determining all those, some of those tasks that you mentioned. There were a lot of questions about what is an essential service, right? Nobody had— there were— we eventually did use a reference list for that but there were always a gray zone We literally would sit down with the, a team of folks at the governor's office, and literally go by business by business sometimes and try to decide where to put them. I want to talk a little bit about what the process was for identifying lab confirmed cases and how the state lab was able to keep up with the volume of people who hope to get tested and what that environment felt like across the state and people in cars driving through test sites. Talk a little bit about what that felt like in the early months of the pandemic. Yes. In the very beginning, it was easy, if you will, because there was no testing, right? That was an easy message, nothing we could do about it. Sorry, this is very distracting [Hahn takes off watch] It beeping at me. In the beginning it was easy because it was like, there is no testing available. We have to go by this symptoms and stay home if you are sick and that kind of thing. What were those symptoms? Really, anybody that had a cough, fever, sore throat, and did not have, let us say a confirmed case of flu. The easy thing was for really, no matter what you are sick with, you should be staying home. We always knew that, but we tended to be much more lax about it, right? But this is a good time to remind people this is what you should be doing anyway if you are sick. But please stay stay home if you are sick at all. It was very challenging almost more complicated once the our state lab got the testing capability from CDC. As you know, there was a lot of controversy about those test kits did not even work properly for everyone. But our state lab— some states got— the CDC put out their test kits like in different batches kind of, and our state lab got a batch that seemed to work just fine. We never identified a problem with it. It performed well. The numbers were small, but they were able to use it. I remember we had a lot of discussions about prioritizing who would qualify for testing. I do not remember the details of how exactly we determined that. But I know that the state lab said, "Here is the priority groups. Here is who we will offer testing for." I think in our state, there was less of a struggle than what I have heard from surrounding states and other states around the country of people, a lot of people, demanding testing. Here I think people just seem to accept for the most part that there just was not a lot of testing available. The testing ended up changing and morphing over time— Yes. — Too, right? Can you talk a little bit about how that process maybe progressed over the course of the pandemic? Yes. First of all, in the beginning our state lab was the only lab in state that had the test kit. Really, we sort of controlled, if you will, not by choice but because there just was not any other testing available. We really had to kind of decide who would get tested the state lab. I know those folks— there are one of the unsung heroes of this whole thing, because they worked so hard to rapidly set up these tests and to run them. They were coming in every weekend running tests. I remember getting calls from providers about desperate situations, somebody who, let us say, had a weak immune system or something and they were really worried about him. Having them bring the samples directly over to our state lab and getting it run on a Saturday. We had a lot of those kind of conversations early on. Once testing got rolled out to more commercial labs, that pressure eased and our state lab took on the role of testing for special populations that may be— that were high risk. For example, nursing homes and prisons and jails, that kind of thing where people were in congregate settings and there might be a big outbreak. They continue to serve that role throughout the pandemic of serving those special populations. We were able to then direct people to private like clinics and other sites for testing, which I think was, obviously had to happen. Right. Even the timing on some of the test turnaround shifted too. Yes. Was that something that was directed from, I mean, the science behind that must have shifted in a way that allowed for a 24-hour result versus a seven day result, right? Right. Exactly. Yes. It just became, just like any technology, as you move along, the PCR tests became better, quicker. Our lab invested in a lot of machinery. They actually bought lab testing machines. Using that term, you can tell I am not a lab person. [both laugh] 'What do I call the PCR machines that they bought for the VA?" If I remember correctly, or they helped the VA obtain one. They placed them in other labs around the state. Really building capacity— Boise State. Building that testing capacity became one of their main— instead of just doing the testing themselves— building testing capacity became a big shift for them. That allowed people to go locally or get the test done locally instead of having it mailed to our lab and then having that delay. Got you. Great. Did, and this might be kind of a broader question, but as we were monitoring our case numbers once testing was available, did that impact how the state received funding or support from the federal government or from other entities? Were you or was the Department of Health and Welfare involved with any of those decisions? Yes. We, of course, started receiving a lot of money from the CDC mostly. There were other sources, but at least here in public health, most of our funding came directly from CDC. Those were, to my recollection, they were not related to how many cases we had because I think they realized that every state needed to build up infrastructure so quickly and really going from zero to a hundred. [Hahn touches watch] I am going to just— this is my— I thought I charged this and it is just— I do not know how to turn it off so I am going to just bury it. I think that the amount of money we were getting, to my knowledge, was not related to our case numbers. They wanted to see that we had to demonstrate that we were doing case counting and reporting it to CDC and, how many cases we had had and that kind of thing. They wanted to know that we had the capacity to do that. And then the funding formulas that CDC developed— Of course, they decide, but myself and other people in this division have good relationships with CDC. We were on a lot of calls discussing how Idaho, even small— we would advocate for small states saying we do not have the same numbers of people, but we need to build the same kind of infrastructure as the big states. We were able, I think us and other rural health, rural states were able to advocate for pretty good funding just so we could get the job done. My next question is about how you served as a public figure throughout the pandemic. Really throughout 2020, 2021, and even throughout part of 2022, you appeared on several television interviews, on weekly Department of Health and Welfare briefings, radio shows, and even throughout social media live streams. I was hoping you could talk a little bit about what it was like to be a public figure in this way for the department and what it was like to prepare for those. What was the backlash like for people who maybe disagreed with the information that you were trying to put out there? Yes. First of all, it is funny because for twenty some years I have been in this job, right, prior to COVID. I frequently have done media interviews or appeared on those same kind of programs. But I always felt like I was kind of the talking head. I was not a personal interest to anybody. It was like, "Oh, somebody on the TV was saying we have got salmonella at the restaurant down the street, right." They do not— who I was did not matter. I truly was just conveying a message and that was always what I thought public health should be like. We were there to provide the message hopefully they say, "Oh, that lady seems like she knows what she is talking about." Hopefully we are trustworthy and reliable, but it does not matter who I am personally. To me, the biggest— when COVID started and we started doing media interviews and press briefings, I felt very comfortable with that because we have always done that. But what was new and different was that the attention became— First of all, because of how many it was and how concerned people were about COVID— Of course, people start paying more attention to the messenger then. I have to say, through all the years of doing the COVID, the last several years, I give a lot of kudos to Idahoans. We certainly had people that disagreed with a lot of what we said. We certainly got emails and phone calls and messages in other ways, responses on social media that people did not agree with us, or even sometimes outright felt like we were deceiving them. But I never personally felt threatened or attacked as a person. Like, "Oh, maybe I did not see some of that stuf." But I never felt that way. I felt like Idahoans, I think that most of them felt like, "Well, she may be misguided, but she seems to believe what she is saying." I never felt like somebody— I think of the kind of the equivalent at the federal level or poor Dr. Fauci, who I met him when he was in the HIV branch of NIH years ago when I was at Duke doing HIV, I mentioned I was in Tanzania looking, doing HIV vaccine research, and he was in the HIV branch. I met him briefly during those days and thought, "Oh, what a smart, nice man." I mean, I never idea, of course, that in the future he would become a household name. I was very surprised to see the vitriol— that he kind of went from being a hero to suddenly being a target. Of course I worried. Will that happen at the state levels? Will I be attacked personally? I never felt like that happened here. Thankfully that allowed me to continue to feel comfortable doing a lot of media press briefings. The strange thing for me was being recognized publicly, which I realized that I do not like it. [both laugh] You never know. You think about people who— I have always wanted to be a celebrity. You walk down the street, and for me it was like I would have people go, "Yoo hoo! Is that— Are you Dr. Hahn?" [Hein laughs] I would be like, "Oh, no." I always worry. I never had, of all the times that, because people did sometimes recognize me in a store or on a street or whatever. People only ever thanked me or said nice things. Now I know I was lucky. There are other people that if they had had a chance probably would have said something more negative, but I never got a negative direct face-to-face feedback from anybody. Again, I certainly had emails from people disagreeing with what we were doing or feeling like I was misguided or worse, but I never felt threatened. I felt in the end that Idaho did a good job with the messengers that were used, if you will, in addition to myself. There were other people obviously talking a lot to the media, and I felt like we did a pretty good job of avoiding what I heard happened in some other states with public officials resigning or being forced out or having some really unpleasant confrontations, people being picketed at their home and that kind of thing. I think Director Jeppesen— I do not know if you are talking to him. I imagine you might be. Yes. He, I believe, did have some picketers at his home. He might have a little different angle on that, but that was pretty unusual. I think for most people that did not happen. Great. The next question is in line— it is about similar means of communication. You ended up giving weekly Facebook Live Q&A sessions, right at the beginning of the pandemic in April and throughout early May of 2020, where the public was able to ask pressing questions about what was going on. In these Facebook Live streams, Director Jepppesen partook, as did Dr. Katherine Turner, Sarah Leeds, who was the manager of Idaho's immunization program, Elke Shaw-Tullock, who is administrator of the Division of Public Health, and yourself. I was wondering if you could talk about how you have seen the shift over time with how the department and your division have communicated with Idahoans about communicable disease, infectious disease, and the role that social media has played in the immediacy of these diseases and the challenge of them, and whether it has been successful or not. Yes, that is a really great question. I think that is one of the big things that was all changing right during COVID, right? I think about—I think this is where this really manifested, to me, the big rub was on our data. One thing that I still think about that will stay with me forever is in the beginning, and in particular, the Idaho Statesmen, I will just call them out, they just seem to really struggle with our data, that it was not being updated quickly enough, it was not accurate. They thought they were seeing changes in numbers. Dr. Turner in particular, who helps create that data dashboard, spent a lot of time defending— "This is why this happened, and the health districts are independent. We get the data from them and then we have to do X, Y, Z." Anyway, there was a lot of discussion about our data quality. I think about the fact that our systems and our processes were built on annual reports. That is all people usually care about, like, "Oh, how many cases of TB did you have last year and how many this year?" That is our normal pace, right, for most things. Certainly in an outbreak, we are used to being more nimble and quick, but usually an outbreak is ten cases of this or maybe, heaven forbid, a hundred cases of that. Not tens of thousands of cases. Our systems, and this of course played out at the national level at the federal level as well, is our system was not built for daily updates of a very large outbreak like this, a pandemic. It is not, I do not think it is lack of foresight on our part. It takes the will, the funding and the know-how to make all that happen. We do not have that. We have never had that. The feeling was that annual reports are probably about what public health needs. When something has not happened in since 1918, it is hard to blame society for not having that foresight and having those systems in place. I think that is one of the things that is coming out of this pandemic is we are seeing now a lot of funding going towards what they are calling the DMI or data modernization initiative. A lot of talk— I just was on a call yesterday with CDC, and they were presenting to the states and talking about the DMI to work on integrating data systems. But for the beginning of the pandemic in 2020, we were suddenly—We do have, fortunately in large part to some of the work of Dr. Turner to get this working, almost all of our lab reports come in electronically. When I started here twenty years ago, most of them came in by fax. Wow. You would get a fax of, let us say a case of salmonella would come in on a fax and then they would look at it, and then they would fax it out to the local health department. They would pick it up off the fax machine, go, "Well, we better call Suzy and talk to her about a salmonella where she got it." Then they would enter it into a DOS system database, very old school database. That had been changed in the late 90s to a more, a little bit more modern system. And then more and more electronic labs were coming in and populating our system. We were on the way. We were getting there. We were not living in the stone ages. But nothing prepared us to be ready for this huge flood of lab reports coming in, a huge flood of demands from the media for daily updates. The numbers would change, which they hated that. We might hear about ten possible cases, lab reports would come in. Let us say we posted that 10am, the district would go and talk to one of the ten and say, "Oh, actually, Suzy, I will use Suzy as my case, Suzy actually lives in Wyoming. She was just visiting here in Boise. The lab report came in from this local lab, but we cannot count her as an Idaho case. She actually lives in Wyoming, so we then transfer the case out. The next day they see nine cases. Like, "Wait a minute, you told us that you had ten cases. Where did that case go?" The numbers change. The faster you try to get numbers out, the more unreliable they are, right? Because you have not vetted them yet, you have not cleaned them. We were used to having a little more time. We had that constant tension between we want to put out the best final data we can, but you guys want it as quick as possible. How do we make that work so that we are as accurate as possible? But there is a tendency to try to nitpick tiny changes or pretty minor changes, then you feel like the headlines become how data is unreliable, and then you lose public trust. It became a very, very challenging and huge part of our early response was getting our data systems set up to get reliable data out that was as timely as possible and was not going to change. But quite a challenge. Yes. Wow. That was one of the main things that stuck with me as far as how the response really changed. Again, like you say, I think with social media. I think about my kids compared to me. My kids will text me at work and expect me, instant answer, right? Whereas I would never have called my father at work. I do not think I ever did that in my you know childhood because he is busy. I wait unitl he gets home. "Wait until your father gets home" kind of thing. I think the expectation— I use that as a little example of how everybody expects instant information now. It is not just the media expecting it. They are doing that because their audience expects it. I understand that it is all the way down the line. The expectation has shifted, but the data systems had not. Wow. Yes. Yes. That was an earful, but that is obviously a huge piece for us. That was a great answer. Thank you so much for all of the nuance in there. That was great. We are going to move forward into 2021. At this point, vaccinations had become available. Those were rolling out in the spring and early summer of 2021. In February of that year, you did a radio interview with Texas Public Radio's Here and Now Program to discuss Idaho's vaccine rollout challenges and the possible solutions. Do you remember doing this interview? I do. Tanya Mosley— Yes. —was lovely. Great. Yes, I remember that interview. When she asked you about Idaho's, Idahoans vaccine hesitancy, you said, quote, "We have a lot of work to do to educate people to give them confidence in this vaccine and to try to overcome the hesitancy and reluctance to get them vaccinated." I was hoping you could talk a little bit about some of the strategies that Idaho used to promote the vaccine, and if you think that those strategies were successful and why? Yes, the strategies we tried to be— aim every which way. I always think that people respond to different things and that took me a long time to figure out. I respond to data. You show me a curve of the higher their vaccination rate, the safer you are. I, "Oh my goodness, I will follow the data and I just act on it." Whereas many people want to see something much more personal. They want to hear a story. They want to have talked to their neighbor who got the vaccine and said, "Oh, it was no problems and I feel great. I was around some of the COVID and I did not get COVID. I think it is because I am..." They need to hear that. They need to have a closer validation. We realized that one size does not fit all. Some people want to talk to their doctor about it. Some people are fine getting information from a government website, if you will. We tried to go on all directions at once. We had the COVID Vaccine Advisory Committee in place at that point. When the vaccines became available in very late 2020, we pulled that Vaccine Advisory Committee together. One of the things we asked them was, how do we get this information out to all the different stakeholders? That committee included people from like the Hispanic community, from the medical community, from people that worked with folks in nursing homes, people that worked with the homeless or disadvantaged populations. We tried to have those people at the table and say, first of all, part of your role in this committee is to help us get the information out through your channels, but also advise us on how to make sure we are getting the information out in a way that is not offensive, that does not ignore certain segments of the population, that is language and culturally appropriate and so forth. I think we tried to have, obviously, press releases, our old traditional ways, website, press release, and so forth. But also have some of our partners go out and do one-on-one education, health fairs, that kind of thing, talk about immunizations. Because we realized that there is no one strategy. As far as how successful we were, our rates did lag behind the rest of the country, but I think if you look at our rates compared to like similar states, other Western states, other very rural states, I felt like we did okay. I often look to Utah, our neighbor to the south, and in some ways they did so well that we did try to look and see what Utah was doing and how can we replicate some of that, some of their techniques. They had some really good, and I do not remember the details of them, but innovative approaches that we tried to replicate here. We did look around to see what our neighbor states were doing, especially the ones that were successful. When you were serving on the Vaccine Advisory Committee, do you remember in developing that plan for, again, which groups were going to receive the first available doses and how you were going to prioritize that rollout. Was that process contentious at all? You would think so. You would think that when something is very scarce, the vaccines came out— I think I just wrote down today, I think our first allocation was 13,000 doses or something. It is not very many for a population of well over a million. I thought we would have a lot of contention and people fighting over that. But the very earliest groups were pretty readily agreed upon, which was health care workers. Everybody knew by now— first of all, clearly essential workers and people that were helping work with COVID patients. I think there was just a lot of support for healthcare workers. And then our other group was the senior citizens, especially in long-term care. I think that really— I think they had such a high risk of dying. There was pretty— I felt like my recollection was they were— first of all, they are always congenial conversations. I do not recall any unpleasantness on that committee. They were all public meetings and people were, a lot of people were on those calls and listening in. I do not think there was ever anything, people angry or yelling at each other or, there were obviously discussions. But I think that first group especially there was a lot of consensus around that. Do you feel like members on the committee deferred more to you in your role as state epidemiologist than others on the committee? Yes, I tried. It would be interesting to hear if other people have, how they weigh in on this, but I felt like I tried to not take an authority role on that advisory committee. We were there. I have the advantage. I think I might have mentioned in the beginning, maybe not, but I participate in CDC's Advisory Committee for Immunization Practices. That committee, we tried to model our committee on that committee. Since I serve on that committee, I felt like, "okay, I am now in that role of the CDC, if you will, listening to my advisory committee. I felt like our role on the Idaho's committee was to listen because we were the agency that ultimately can decide and make the decisions. But the advisory committee really should be telling us their thoughts. And then we can guide the questions and try to learn a little bit more. But I think we tried very hard not to be heavy-handed. Of course, we sometimes had to create a proposal for people to react to. But I think there were a lot of strong opinion, confident and opinionated people on the committee, which we included on purpose. Right. We wanted people that could speak their mind and were confident. I do not think that I ever felt like anybody was deferring to our thoughts on it. We tried to be as neutral as we could so that they could tell us their thoughts. Great. [pause] You also served on the Crisis Standards of Care Committee, which pulled together their Crisis Standards of Care plan in June of 2020. So far in the course of this project, you were the first person I have spoke to who served on this particular committee. If my memory serves me correct, we had to go into crisis standards of care three times I think over the course of the pandemic. Yes, I was reviewing that myself today. I was like, "what exactly happened?" I think the way it happened was that there was a one time we were in and out, and another time we were in partway and then expanded. Yes. So three separate declarations. Yes. Talk to me about what that committee's purpose was, what it was like to develop that plan and then if you can remember what it was like in those moments when the hospitals really had to implement and execute the plan that you developed. Yes, so first of all I think I am not the only one in the Division of Health or the department that would say, we should have had that plan in place a long time ago. It was something that the CDC had been asking the states to put in place to create these plans. Through various— not for lack of trying. We had been working on trying to get a crisis standards of care, but you can imagine how difficult that conversation is, how legal concerns about can you even do this. We kept getting stuck in that plan. It should have been in place long before 2020, but it was not. I need to thank and acknowledge Dr. Marcia Witte, who you have probably come across her name, but she and Dr. Hamso from Medicaid worked very hard behind the scenes and with another committee— there was the activation committee that I was on, but there was also a planning committee— They really hammered through and got something put together, got the Board of Health and Welfare to sign off on some rules that would allow crisis and secure to be put in place, worked with the legal folks to kind of make sure we were square. That had to be done very quickly, unfortunately, during the pandemic. It was very frustrating, but I understand that sometimes there is not the will to move things forward until you really need to. That said, I thought the system and process worked very well once it was in place. I thought that was a success for the state, because we coupled that, and really under the director's guidance and leadership, coupled that with regular calls with the hospitals like, "How are you doing? How is it going? Are you running out of beds? What are your needs?" The hospitals were the ones that would ask for crisis stands of care, really. The director would respond to their requesting, because they felt like they could not possibly go on any longer the way they were without some leeway. I think that whole process ended up working quite well. And then having an activation committee, with the hospitals requesting the process, the activation committee considering and making like a declaration, I think, allowed the hospitals a little bit of breathing room so they could get through the crisis. Now I cannot remember if you asked me a specific question about the... Just about if your memory, or if you can remember or reflect on those kind of three phases when the state had to go into those standards of care and implement. Yes, in my mind the first one was the most dramatic probably because it was the first one and it was unprecedented in the state. We knew it had never happened before because we had created this process that there was no precedent for. That was very dramatic. To a certain extent, I worried that the governor or other leadership would view that as a failure. The media would report— there were a few other states that declared crisis standards of care around the country. You felt like it was saying, "We cannot help. We were not doing things right." Where I felt like it was a tool that we used well to help the hospitals get through and the public get through the crisis. I was proud of our state for declaring the crisis standards of care. I think there were many other states that could have and maybe should have and there was either not the plan in place or a reluctance to do so. I remember feeling very dramatic because it made national headlines. I think we were the third state in the country to do so, I believe, and it felt like, because of that national attention, it felt very dramatic. That said, It always felt like it was the right way to go, and I felt like we helped get the state through these bumpy waters. The second time around, which I think was during Omicron, so I think Omicron kind of hit, but that felt like, "Okay, we have already been here before. We know what this is going to do for the hospitals." The fact that they requested again to me was a validation that it was helpful to them. I thought again, it felt more normal almost the next time. Like, "Okay, we know that this is the way to get through this crisis." Fortunately, Omicron was a short, short crisis. I remember, I did my timeline, we were not in that crisis, second crisis standards of care for very long. Right. Excellent. Thank you. We are going to jump forward to 2022. You did an interview with Doug Petcash from KTVB Channel 7, where he actually asked you to reflect on a two-year timeline of the pandemic. He asked you specifically about some of the early challenges that the state faced, and you responded saying, "You cannot respond to something that you cannot measure or test for. And that was a huge setback. The delay in testing was a setback, and we thought we would have clearer guidance on masking." Do you remember giving this interview with Doug Petcowicz? I do not remember that particular one because, bless his heart, he had us on a lot, which was wonderful. Yes. He really wanted to spread the word so I did multiple interviews with him. I do not remember that particular interview but I believe I said that. [laughter] Okay. I was hoping that you could talk a little bit about aside from the relationship directly with the CDC, more broadly about what it was like for you to watch what was happening at the national level and what was happening at the state level. Just reflect on the distinctions or the similarities and where you found challenge or opportunity in that space? Yes, yes. Great question. In some ways, of course, we felt like we were all going through this whole we are all in this together kind of feeling of everybody has the same thing. But what was interesting to me is it felt like we were offset time-wise. A lot of times the national data would say, "Oh my gosh! Cases are up up up!" We would not be seeing that. And then maybe several weeks later we would see it or something like that. We were offset. I think a lot of that was because a lot of the waves seemed to start in the East Coast. We would hear about these huge—of course, New York City, we all remember that was one of the early places that got hit really hard. In some ways we felt like we were out of step. There would be these big national headlines saying, cases are at an all-time high, the hospitals are full. People would call us and would be like, "Well, it is not so bad here. We are okay." But then a few weeks later, we might be seeing that rise. And then it felt like, "Well, gee, the news is already, we already know that cases are high, so we don't necessarily want to talk about Idaho being high because..." We felt like that added a little bit to the challenge that we were offset. The other big difference I noticed was the, really, to a certain extent, the public's attitude. We would hear and even get questions from our neighbor either from the West Coast or the East Coast saying, "Oh my gosh. Everybody is demanding vaccines or everybody is demanding this and we would be like, well, here we are not hearing as much about"— We certainly have people that are interested in this or concerned about that, but it felt like a different intensity level compared to what we were hearing from other places. Just the cultural differences, I think, the morality, people being more maybe independent minded versus looking to the state government to get— provide for them. We felt like we had a different, we could really see, it was really highlighted or brought into focus the differences in some of the populations where to a certain extent I think that helped us because it allowed us to be a little more— we did not have the constant pressure of people constantly calling and demanding, "Why are you not doing this? Why are you not doing that?" I felt like we were allowed to do what we wanted to do. Of course, it also, you could say, could have limited us because in some states they instantly put mandate—mandatory this, mandatory that, whether it was getting vaccine, wearing masks. Whether it was mandated closures, which of course, as you mentioned early on, early on that happened, but we quickly opened up and the governor became very proud of the fact that we were one of the first states opening up. So we would have neighbor states where they were still very shut down saying and publicly saying "Those Idahoans are spreading it everywhere because they're all free to go wherever they want." You got into the state by state thing, which was you probably in your research saw Governor Inslee from Washington State made some negative comments about Idaho several times. You had to kind of weather that storm a little bit. We also found ourselves explaining to our federal partners, you can only mandate something if people are willing to do it. We do not, we are not going to station a policeman at every door to make sure people do not leave their homes. Nobody is going to arrest people in the street for not wearing a mask. If we are not going to mandate it, if we are not going to enforce it, I guess is what I am trying to say. If we are not going to enforce it and we cannot enforce something like that, you have to have the public will and support of the people to do it. If we do not sense that is the case, we are wasting our time and we're also showing that we're out of step with the public. We felt like we went as far as we would be acceptable and accepted by Idahoans and actually have ultimately a better outcome, which is to strongly recommend what we thought was common sense measures, right? We recommend you get vaccinated. We recommend you wear a mask. But other than the very beginning, when so much was unknown, there were much fewer mandates than other states. I was just at a meeting, I know we are talking about 2022, but I was just at a meeting about a month ago with some other state epidemiologists and it was Washington and Oregon saying, "Yes, we are thinking of stopping our statewide mask mandate for health care workers and we were and they are like "How did that work for you and I said we never we never mandated it for health care workers." Institutions did, right? The big hospitals here, St. Luke's and St. Al's just rescinded that a month ago or so. But they did it for themselves. We did not issue a statewide mandate of any type. We definitely saw that we had different approaches around the country, and to a certain extent, I think we, I think, did our best to read the public and try to get the best outcome we could based on what we thought the public would support and do. Yes, I think that is in alignment with the culture that has existed here in Idaho for a long time. Yes. We have reached the last section of the questionnaire and of the interview, which is again a chance for you to just kind of give some, you know, 30, 000 level foot view reflections. But I do have one question that is maybe a little bit more personal in nature. I was hoping you could talk a little bit about how COVID impacted work that you, I think, were doing before the pandemic set in and that you continued to do throughout, which is work that you were carrying on at the tuberculosis clinic here in Boise. I was hoping you could talk just a little bit about what that work was and is and then how how COVID impacted that place because I think it would be really interesting to talk about an infectious disease and the impact of another infectious disease on that infectious disease? Yes. That is a great question. Actually worldwide it is still an open question on whether ultimately COVID was good or bad for people regarding TB. Meaning, to a certain extent, people that were sick with TB, you think, "well, goodness, if they couldn't go in and get diagnosed and treated, how terrible." On the other hand, if everybody is wearing a mask, a high quality mask, especially in countries that where the population can afford that, you are actually slowing the spread of TB. I think that has not been finally decided yet. There is some early indications, case rates went down, but you would expect that just because people cannot get diagnosed. I think now we are waiting to have the other shoe drop and see if ultimately rates go up again. That is a really interesting question, especially because they are both respiratory diseases and because TB use those, that is where a lot of the COVID responses, we learned a lot from TB because we are used to wearing the N95s for TB. Some of the other techniques and things that we did for isolation and quarantine, those laws are in place largely to protect people from TB nowadays. That is what we use them for. At any rate, there was a lot of great overlap there. I think for our TB clinic, the way it affected us most was first of all for a while, if I remember this correctly, first of all we all wore masks all the time. We used to only wear a mask at the TB clinic if we were seeing somebody with active TB, we always make sure that they are as safe as possible. They are on treatment and they should be non-infectious, but just to be on the safe side we will all wear our masks. That changed to suddenly were all wear masks, right, because it is— we are in a health care setting. For a while we were only seeing people virtually too. It was all telehealth type visits. I really miss seeing people in person and I think you lose a lot. I mean I respect the need for telehealth but I find it much less satisfying than seeing people in person. I think sometimes you pick up things in person that you would not see telehealth wise. You see how they walk into the room. You see who they bring with them. Do they have support, right? Do they have a spouse or a child that is... You learn a lot from those in-person visits. For a while we were doing telehealth only, and I think some of the patients too were clearly depressed and isolated. TB is already isolating, right? You have to isolate, but usually after a few weeks of treatment, as long as you stay on treatment, you can get back out into the public again. You can go back to work. You just keep taking your antibiotics, but you are no longer infectious. Now they were stuck at home and they were sick, and it was a very, very difficult time. I had one TB patient, I will never forget him, an elderly gentleman. We got him through his TB and I remember having that last visit with him and it was congratulations and this was a rough go but you made it through. I want to say about a month later he died of COVID. It just broke my heart because I felt like he had just been so stalwart. The older people, these medications can be tough on them. He was isolated for a while. He had his daughter living with him, helping him out at home. But he was getting his strength back. I am sure the TB set him up for that because his lungs were already kind of damaged. That sort of interplay between TB and COVID, that just was a really hit home for me with that case. Thank you for sharing. You hold a pretty high position here at the Department of Health and Welfare. What do you think you learned about yourself as a leader over the last three years? Yes. That is a great question. I think, like I said, I felt like "oh, I already do all these things You know, I do briefs. I do whatever." I what I learned was a couple things. I felt like in a way I Held up, you know, I made it through I I did not quit. I did not you know, I did not say could somebody else please take on these duties. I felt like I did okay. On the other hand, a couple things I did learn. One is I do not like the public, the limelight if you were the public eye. It got me more respectful of the people that do not mind going out there and saying the controversial things and having people angry at them and having people send them nasty emails or give them calls. I do not want to be that. I have a family. My most important thing to me is my family, and I did not want to, the thought of, I have two, you know, kids at home, and the thought of somebody showing up at my door and protesting or whatever, I just, that to me would not have been worth it. I would have said, no, I am not, I cannot do this to them. So I learned that even though I can kind of hopefully step up in a crisis, I do not want to be that that face in a controversial situation that everybody decides they hate. That is just not something I think I could do well at. I also learned again I think of I learned a lot from Director Jepson who I think you said you will be talking to. That man never got flustered, never got angry, just has the most calm, cool, I do not want to say cool like in a negative way, but just a very calming presence and always seemed to absorb whatever was happening and then thoughtfully respond. I felt like I learned a lot from him during the, I sort of tried to model in some ways as far as your bearing and how you respond in pressure situations. I felt like I learned a lot from him during the— I sort of tried to model in some ways as far as your bearing and how you respond in pressure situations. I felt like I learned a lot from him. Great. You mentioned you have a family and children at home. What was the pandemic like for you in that setting? Yes, it was interesting. I felt like, we all heard stories, whether through the media or our friends about, "Oh, I stayed home for three months and learned how to crochet and speak French." I felt like if anything, I was spending more time away from my family and away from home because I did continue to come into the office almost throughout, as I am sure you will hear from others. A lot of people went home, but for those of us that had to meet daily— Sometimes we did in person, sometimes it was on Zoom, but a lot of us, we felt better if we were here in the office. It was more connected and more— a better place to be, fewer distractions and all that. For me, I felt like it was a period of time where they saw their mom less and my husband saw me less. But on the other hand, I could go home every night. My kids did fine through the pandemic. My son decided to do a year of the online school when that was offered, which I am not sure I would let him do that again. He sort of liked being in his room with the door closed a little too much. I definitely— I think the one thing I did see from him and my own family was I think it was very tough on the young kids. I think us adults, we struggled with it too, no doubt. But I saw my kids where they missed out on all these opportunities that we all look back on from school opportunities of the dances, the sport events, whatever. What a loss for them. You will never get back. And then the isolation. I think since I saw that in my family, I think it also gave me that empathy with other people. Whether I thought it was the right thing to do to shut down, let us say, sporting events. I understood the strong passion that those things should have been allowed to continue as best as they could. I think this one area of the state did pretty well of trying to allow those as best they could. You alluded to this a little bit in your answer about leadership, but, and how some people decided to come into the office for that point of connection. But how do you think the agency, Health and Welfare, one of the larger state agencies here in state government in Idaho, managed this given that they really were in the limelight throughout? I have talked with other directors of other agencies. I have talked with Labor, I have talked with Commission on Aging, I have talked with the Division of Financial Management, I am talking with others. But public health, Health and Welfare, they were in the limelight. Does the agency feel stronger now than it did before? Yes. I have to say a couple things. I will once again say I think we have had— I have been here through many directors and they have all been good in many ways and had their strengths, but Director Jeppson really has to get a lot of credit for bringing the agency through with credibility and with minimal— we had headlines saying our data was not this or we had our issues and challenges, but I felt like the other one was the vaccine rollout. Of course, getting the vaccine out, getting the vaccine data up also was a separate little bump of trying to get that data out quickly. But I felt like the department came through it stronger, and I think without the loss of credibility. There is some people say, "oh, health and welfare, I have never trusted them, and now I really do not." I think there are some people that never— had a negative opinion to start. But I think as far as people who had a neutral or positive impression before the pandemic, I do not think we lost a lot of that faith that maybe we saw from CDC. Knowing that you have had experience in really across the world responding to public health crises, how do you think Idaho handled this pandemic in comparison to those other experiences you have had? Yes. Honestly, not everything went the way I would if I had the magic wand that I would have done it. But ultimately, I believe in our system and I think it worked. The people spoke, they let us know what they thought. I think it did influence policy. We also had advisory committees and groups that weighed in that had a lot of say so and ended up influencing policy. I think the governor and the director heard from a lot of people, business leaders, the legislature—they had a special session, as you know. I think that all messy as it felt and contentious as it could get here and there, I feel like it worked. Ultimately, if you believe in democracy, you have to believe that that is the way to go. Instead of, I had people say, "why do you guys not just demand this or require this? I am like, I just do not think that is what the people want. I do not think that is the right thing to do, considering as we talked about earlier, you can try to sit there and be heavy handed, but you are not gonna get anywhere. I feel like we, I think due to the wisdom of the leadership, I feel like we ended up in about the right place. I saw a study pretty recently, trying to look at state by state just if you try to figure out what's what is success right I think there is a couple ways to measure it. One would be just death rates by age obviously with senior population if you adjust for age and things like that. Looking at just mortality rates, hospitalization rates, how did we do that way? You can look at economy, you can look at things like, "okay, at the end of the day, how is the faith in the structure shaken?" I feel like in all those measures that we did pretty well, and this study was particularly, I think, looking at mortality. It sounds like we were kind of middle of the pack there. There were states that did better. I think many of those were states with very high immunization rates. But there were a lot of states that did worse and I felt like we did the best we could. Great. We have reached the last question on my questionnaire and I end every interview with this one, which is really, Is there anything that I should have asked you that I did not? You know, I will say one thing, which is that kind of how do you think this will be all remembered? I think about 1918 flu, which was always our benchmark, right, for pandemics. I never heard a word about it till I was studying medicine. I did not learn about it in school. I did not learn about it in college. I did not learn about it even in medical school. I think it was in infectious disease training that I started to— Maybe they mentioned it briefly in medical school, but in a way that it did not stay with me. I feel like in the same way I worry that this pandemic, and that is why I really appreciate what you are doing, I think this pandemic potentially is going to be forgotten too, because it's fresh in our minds. We just went through it and we're already adults when it's happening. But like I feel like my kids, they are going to tell their grandkids all back in, you know, whatever we had to stay home from school for. Yeah, grandma, whatever. I just feel like history is that we tend to forget unpleasant things, we put it behind us. I do worry that the pandemic will be forgotten. I think the way to remember it is to make changes right away while people remember, like the date of modernization, things like that, and to record what happened, because I do think it will— I think in three, five years we will be amazed at how much is forgotten. Yes. I know as part of Governor Little's request for information to help make decisions, he came to our agency and I worked on a policy paper for his office and one of the outcomes of the Spanish flu here in Idaho was the requirement to register nurses. Oh, interesting. To your point about making decisions immediately, the legislature in 1921 changed the licensing laws that required that that term "RN" to be appended to anybody who was licensed to to be a nurse in the state and they tracked it much tighter after the pandemic here in Idaho because they could not keep track of where their nurses were. Oh interesting. That, at least from my research, was the biggest impact of the Spanish flu here in Idaho was the change to that law. Oh that is so interesting. Which they made within a year. Yes, interesting. Yes. I do think what I saw and you probably just saw too in the legislature was for the moment still kind of the opposite, right? The sort of the negative taking away authorities from public health and making it a crime to administer an mRNA vaccine and kind of some of that. But I am hoping that long term, in the near term, but I hope it swings back to some positive changes, and I think those are hopefully already happening. Yes, me too. Thank you so much for your time. Sure. I will go ahead and pause the recorder. Okay.

lines.line: # Introduction and Background

> HannaLore Hein introduces herself and the interviewee, Dr. Christine Hahn, and explains the purpose of the interview. Dr. Hahn discusses her educational background and career path leading up to her role as Idaho State Epidemiologist.

metadata.title: Introduction and Background
metadata.synopsis: HannaLore Hein introduces herself and the interviewee, Dr. Christine Hahn, and explains the purpose of the interview. Dr. Hahn discusses her educational background and career path leading up to her role as Idaho State Epidemiologist.

lines.line: # Early Career and Public Health Interest

> Dr. Hahn shares her early career experiences, including her fellowship at the CDC and her decision to focus on public health over clinical practice. She describes the work she did on infectious disease outbreaks and her transition to public health after working in Tanzania.
** CDC, public health, infectious diseases, Tanzania, career transition **
keywords: ** CDC, public health, infectious diseases, Tanzania, career transition **

story.title: COVID-19 Oral History with Christine Hahn
story.description: In this interview, Dr. Christine Hahn, Idaho State Epidemiologist, discusses her experiences and challenges during the COVID-19 pandemic. She describes her career path, emphasizing her transition from clinical medicine to public health due to a keen interest in infectious diseases. Dr. Hahn outlines her involvement in managing outbreaks and her responsibilities as an epidemiologist, particularly focusing on her role during the pandemic. She reflects on Idaho's response to COVID-19, covering topics like vaccination rollout, public communication, and data management. Dr. Hahn also highlights the personal impact of the pandemic and its effects on tuberculosis management, sharing insights on leadership, public health policies, and future pandemic preparedness.
